title: Detroit to Berlin
description: The transatlantic axis that defined modern electronic music. From Motown's machine-funk to the cavernous clubs of reunified Berlin.
author: DiscoWorld

waypoints.note: Where it all began — Juan Atkins, Derrick May, Kevin Saunderson inventing a new sound from Kraftwerk, funk, and the future.
The common ancestor. Electro's robotic grooves fed both Detroit and the European scene.
The sound crystallizes. 4/4 kicks, hypnotic sequences, machines talking to machines.
Berlin's answer: strip it down to the essential. Robert Hood, Richie Hawtin, the Basic Channel axis.
Where Detroit's soul meets Berlin's space. Endless reverb, submerged chords, time dissolving.
The industrial edge. Berlin warehouses push the tempo and the distortion.

title: Birth of House
description: From Chicago's South Side to global domination. The story of house music in six chapters.
author: DiscoWorld

waypoints.note: The mother. Before house was house, it was disco records played in Chicago warehouses.
Frankie Knuckles at the Warehouse. Ron Hardy at the Music Box. A drum machine and a dream.
Phuture's 'Acid Tracks' — the TB-303's squelchy revolution. The sound that crossed the Atlantic.
The soulful side. Larry Heard's 'Can You Feel It' — house music with jazz chords and emotion.
New York's interpretation. Paradise Garage, Larry Levan, the diva vocal, the breakdown.
The 90s evolution. Longer builds, deeper journeys, stadium moments.

title: UK Bass Evolution
description: The uniquely British obsession with bass weight, from jungle to dubstep to grime and beyond.
author: DiscoWorld

waypoints.note: The original. Breakbeats at 170 BPM, massive bass, ragga vocals. Pirate radio gold.
Jungle grows up. Goldie, LTJ Bukem, Photek refine the formula.
Speed garage loses the beats, gains the swing. MJ Cole, Artful Dodger, the UK charts.
Half-time, full weight. Croydon's finest: Skream, Benga, Digital Mystikz.
MC culture meets garage production. Wiley, Dizzee Rascal, bow to the bass.
The carnival connection. Broken beats, tribal drums, London's multicultural bass music.

title: Ambient Odyssey
description: Music for airports, forests, and the spaces between thoughts. A journey into electronic music's contemplative side.
author: DiscoWorld

waypoints.note: Brian Eno's invitation: 'Music as ignorable as it is interesting.' The starting point.
The shadow side. Lustmord, Coil — ambient music for abandoned factories.
When ambient gets a pulse. Slow, thoughtful, headphone music with a heartbeat.
Bristol's gift. Massive Attack, Portishead — cinematic downtempo with hip-hop bones.
Intelligent Dance Music (that nobody dances to). Aphex Twin, Autechre, Boards of Canada.
The sound of broken machines made beautiful. Oval, Alva Noto, clicks and cuts.

title: Rave History
description: From illegal warehouses to massive festivals. The story of rave culture in tempo and intensity.
author: DiscoWorld

waypoints.note: Summer of Love, 1988. Acid house hits the UK. Smiley faces, warehouse parties, moral panic.
It becomes a movement. Fields, motorways, police helicopters. The Criminal Justice Act awaits.
Rave's sugar rush. Pitched-up vocals, piano stabs, 170 BPM euphoria.
The melodic arm. Hands in the air, eyes closed, lost in the build and release.
When trance gets serious. Harder kicks, bigger drops, festival main stages.
The spiritual wing. Goa beaches, full moon parties, 16-hour outdoor rituals.

title: Industrial Underground
description: The dark side of electronic music. From art-noise provocation to body-moving industrial techno.
author: DiscoWorld

waypoints.note: Throbbing Gristle's 'industrial music for industrial people.' Provocation as art form.
Electronic Body Music. Front 242, Nitzer Ebb — industrial learns to dance.
Gothic electronics. Skinny Puppy, FLA — the sound of beautiful darkness.
The extreme edge. Merzbow, Whitehouse — volume as violence, distortion as liberation.
Where noise meets rhythm. Converter, Imminent — brutal but structured.
Industrial's final form: 4/4 techno at maximum intensity. The Berlin bunker sound.

title: From Disco to Trance
description: How four-on-the-floor evolved from Studio 54 to Ibiza. The longest continuous thread in dance music.
author: DiscoWorld

waypoints.note: 1977. The Bee Gees, Donna Summer, Giorgio Moroder's sequencers. The beat that started everything.
Disco goes electronic. Cheap synths, catchy melodies, European style.
Italy's Hi-NRG offshoot. Fast, cheesy, unstoppable on the dancefloor.
The 90s formula. MC + diva + synth hook = chart domination.
The formula perfected. Long builds, emotional breakdowns, the superclub era.
Trance finds its voice. Above & Beyond, Armin — stadium anthems with feeling.

title: Vinyl Collector's Rarities
description: Deep cuts and forgotten gems from the most sought-after genres. For the crate digger who's heard everything.
author: DiscoWorld

waypoints.note: Early Metroplex, Transmat, KMS pressings. The genesis records.
Rare breaks compilations, white labels, the DJ's secret weapons.
Chain Reaction, Basic Channel — the most collected electronic 12"s on earth.
Café Del Mar, Ibiza sunsets, Jose Padilla's musical postcards.
The most niche of all: Dutch space disco. Laser Dance, Koto. Collectors go deep.
Cosmic disco, the Italian connection. Daniele Baldelli's DJ sets as lost masterpieces.
